speaker: interviewer
text: Today's October 13, 1995. The survivor is Herbert Karliner, K-A-R-L-I-N-E-R. The interviewer is Jacqueline Leone, L-E-O-N-E. The city is Aventura in the Miami, Florida district, USA. The language is English. This will be roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's October the 13th, 1995. The survivor is Herbert Karliner. I'm Jacqueline Leone, and we are in Aventura, Florida. The language will be English. Thank you for being with us today. And if you would please state your name and when and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Herbert Karliner. I was born in Peiskretscham. It's a small city in East Germany near the Polish border. And my date of birth is September 3rd 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about what life was like for you in Germany before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war-- [CLEARS THROAT] --we were-- come from a middle-class family, and we had a small Jewish community in our town. And there was no problem before that-- when Hitler-- before Hitler came to power. But once Hitler came to power, we felt already starting anti-Semitism. We went-- I went to a school, a regular elementary school. And beginning, we didn't have no problem. But around 1937-- '36, '37-- I really felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways did you feel it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, for example, we-- in school when we were-- just to give you a little example--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a movie, and they show you about Hitler, Hitler Youth. And the teacher told us that after the movie, everybody can go home. Well, we all went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the next day, when we came to school, he pulled out all the Jewish boys and said, who told you to go home? He-- I don't know if you know-- in Germany, if you don't behave, you turn around and you get a beating with a bamboo stick on your behind. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened to us. That's one thing. The other thing is, I used to love to play soccer. After school, we always went to play soccer with the other boys. And instead of kick the ball, they used to kick us in the-- on the shin, on the feet, and so on. For a while, we were-- we were not allowed to go to movies anymore. We were not allowed to walk on the sidewalks, because every time we walked on the sidewalks, the other kids kicked us around. Before the war-- or before the-- Hitler came to power, my parents were very highly regarded there from the-- all the other people. We had a very nice business. But once they came there, the whole situation changed. Even the people who came to buy from us were not allowed to come anymore. They, they threatened them. And the business went all the way down.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- a lot of my family left, already, Germany, because they went to Palestine or they went to the United States. My father never thought that something like this will happen, because he felt that-- most of his family and him, himself was in the German army in the First World War. And how can the German-- would do something like this to us? But he was wrong. Do you remember much discussion in the family about this? I mean, was there a lot of conflict with your father's brothers? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, certainly. Certainly. A lot of-- a lot of his brothers or cousins, or whatever it is, told him to leave. But he didn't believe it. He said this will get over that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they left? Many--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they left. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this situation got worse and worse, and then came the Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: November the 9th, 1939-- '38, excuse me. And we had two homes. One was the front home, where the store was, and we slept in the back of the house. My father went down on six o'clock to open the store, and all of a sudden, I hear him screaming, somebody broke into their stores. We all went downstairs, and sure enough, the whole store was completely destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the first you knew about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first we knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't heard it the night before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Nothing. We didn't hear nothing that was happening. So we all went down stairs, and somebody came running to us and said, they-- the synagogue is burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father and myself, we went to the synagogue. And sure enough, there was a fire there, and they made a bonfire with all the prayer books. And even the Torah they threw in the fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father wanted to save the Torah, but the SR, which was the brown shirt at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --They kicked him around and throw him-- I mean, he wasn't allowed to save it. Immediately, somebody else came to us and said, I understand the Gestapo is picking up all the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we run back home, and we didn't know what to do. We waited for the Gestapo to come. Because-- so the Gestapo came, but they picked up only my father-- Only your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: --And put him in prison. So were-- we were-- we didn't know what to do-- what to do, you know? So he was in prison. In the meantime, my aunt from the other city called us and said the same thing happened to them. So I-- my mother sent me by train to the other town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were like, 11 or 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 11 years old. And I found out that all-- my father and the others who were taken from the city-- in a prison that-- in the next town, which was Gleiwitz. I went to that prison, and I saw my father. And everybody gave me-- told me what to say to their family, and so on, and so on. I couldn't remember everything. And I went back to my hometown and I told everybody there, and so everybody went to other town again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were-- you were allowed to see your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In prison. Was there any charges?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No charges whatsoever. When I came back, everybody went to see their father or their whatever there. But when we came back, they were taken away already. They went to Buchenwald. We had to straighten out the, the store. We closed the store, because there-- it was completely destroyed. And the only way to-- well, then that was the end from us to stay in Germany. We felt that we had to go out. My father had tried already before to leave for the United States, and we were on the quota.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were already on the list, so to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the list already. So the only way our people get out of Buchenwald, was that you have to have a permit that you will leave Germany in the next three or six months. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to get my father out, we got a permit to go to China, Shanghai. Then when he came out-- it was about-- he was about two weeks-- two and a half weeks he was in Buchenwald. But when he came out, I did not recognize him. Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shaved him. He was so skinny. And he didn't talk to me about what happened in there, because I think he was not allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not allowed? Or he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not allowed to say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, within himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe he was not-- you know. We didn't want to go to China. In the meantime, we find out there's a Cuban council who's selling permits to go to Cuba. I think we paid $500 for each person. I came from a family of six. We were two boys, two girls, and my parents-- six people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- let's step back a little bit and look at your family. Your sisters were older, younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister was older. My brother was older. I was the third, and my-- I have another sister who was younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was younger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other aunts and uncles who were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- In-- who were taken into the camp who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes certainly. There was some-- two brothers from my sister-- from my sister-- from my mother. From my father, there were about four uncles were also taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community kind of get together and decide what to do as a community? Or did each family try to deal with it the best the could?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each family were-- for the best they could. The Jewish community couldn't do nothing at that time. Anyway, so we bought those permits to go to Cuba, because we felt to go to United States, it will be much easier from Cuba to come to the United States. I had an uncle who was a judge in Germany. And then he, he was thrown out by judgeship, and then he lost also to be a lawyer, and he was not allowed to be a lawyer. And he was also in Buchenwald. And when they released him, he said, I'm not going to wait one more day. The first ship I can take, I get out from Germany. And he took a, a freighter. I think it was the [NON-ENGLISH], who went from Hamburg to Havana. And he left before us. He said--

speaker: interviewer
text: He left before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I don't want to wait with you. I'm going out. And he made it to Havana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a wife and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was, he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was single.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone. Yeah. This was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you gather your things together? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have things to gather at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we had to sell our house. The Gestapo came and they said, this house is worth $10,000, which was ridiculous, you know? And we had to sell it for that price. We had some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they allow you to take out anything you want from Germany, except you have to pay everything double.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to pay everything double?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tax-wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you had a watch, which is- you want to buy for $100, you have to pay $100 tax.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anything-- we were not allowed to take any money out of, of Germany. So that's what we did. For ever we could-- my father made some boxes, which-- in case we get someplace, we just put it against the wall and we can open them up. It was like-- how you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a trunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a trunk, but you know, everything ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: With shelves and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was your father in before?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in-- how you call it-- all-supply, all kind of supply, but also mostly wholesale grain. He bought it from the farmers, and he sold it to the mills.

speaker: interviewer
text: Foodstuffs and things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So-- but my family, my grandparents used to live for a century in that village. They-- my father had 14 brothers and sisters, and they all were born in the small town, my grandfather and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents still alive at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only grandfather I knew was my mother's-- from my mother's side, and he used to live in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is not far away, but I, I knew him. This was in 1939. I'm just trying to think of anything else what happened in Germany before, before we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there speeches on the street? I mean, how were you told what to do? How did you know what to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, how did the Germans say, no more this, no more that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't. They just gave a law that Jews were not allowed to go to the movies. We're not allowed to sit in the bus in the front, or maybe only in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you went, what would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would put you in prison. Yeah, that's how I remember this one thing, another little example. One guy I used to play soccer and was a good friend of mine-- and that was in the wintertime. And I went with my little sister to-- on the sled in the mountains to go down. And he didn't have a sled, and he came by and said, can I go with you? I wanted to be friendly with him. I said sure, come. He went there, and I was in the front of the sled, my sister in the middle, and he was in the back. Down the hill, he started to beat my sister for no reason whatsoever. And I get very angry. When I went down from the hill. I grabbed him and knocked the hell out of him. Well, I was-- I had little satisfaction. The only problem is that at night, the Gestapo came and picked up my father and took him, put him in prison. Jews were not allowed to hit an Aryan. So my father had to go in prison. I don't remember how long he was in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the second time or the same time? This was the-- the first time he went to prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was afterward.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only thing which I didn't like, because after my father came out, I got a licking from him too, yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, I think was not-- I still remember it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You still remember that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I could understand, because my father was very nervous with all those things that were happening. And he didn't want to jeopardize the, the safety for the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your mother dealing with this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very much depressed, because not only herself, but also the brothers of hers were-- one brother was a dentist, the other brother was a, a lawyer in Breslau-- and they were also taken. And she wanted to keep the family together. All of my, my mother's two brothers decided also to go to Cuba. One of them went. I had mentioned to you before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other one took also a permit to Cuba, and we were together. I would say we were about eight, 10 people, relatives, on that ship, plus some other friends also. Well, for me to leave Germany, it was a pleasure, because I always wanted to come to the United States. As far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even as a kid. I was reading some books, German books, about the United States, and I was always fascinated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even before Hitler, was there a lot of talk in the family about leaving Germany? Or was it-- or did everybody expect to stay there forever?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, like I said before, some of the family wanted to get out, and they went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some went to Palestine. Some went to the United States. But my father said, it will pass. It will passe, you know? It didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing, a little example I will gave you-- that we had a lawyer, a Jewish lawyer, [INAUDIBLE], and he was decorated in the First World War. He was a-- Jews were not allowed to be more than a lieutenant in the German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the First World War. But he was wounded and was decorated. He has an iron cross. And when the Gestapo came, he said, you cannot do that to me. I'm a Germany Iron Cross. It was no excuse. They all went the same way. Anyway, we finally left Germany. We packed everything, and we took the train from-- we went Breslau to Berlin-- Berlin, Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: No problems on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. The German government gave a permission to, to take that ship, St. Louis, which was a luxury liner, from the American [INAUDIBLE]-- I think it's a German line-- went New York, Hamburg. It was a very luxurious ship at that time. And they gave us that ship to go to Cuba, with the refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except-- which we never believed or thought of it-- we had to pay round-trip ticket. We never thought this-- things like this happen. Why? I don't know? You mean, in order to buy a ticket, you had to buy a round-trip ticket.

speaker: interviewee
text: A round-trip ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you were only going one way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the-- of what the German government was saying about this ship before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as you were concerned, you were just glad to get on it. Your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. Yes. And even the Germans didn't make much fuss about that at the time. We left Hamburg the 13th of May, 1939. And I was-- oh, I was so happy. And my father-- my parents were very sad, because they'd grown up in there, and there was their home. And an older couple, you know, to start a new life, it's very difficult. But for us children, it was a-- an adventure, if you want to call, or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of children on board?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. We were about 937 passengers, more or less, on that, mostly-German and things. There was a couple from Czechoslovakia too, or something like that. They-- the accommodations were very good. The food was excellent. The service, also very good from the ship crew, and so on. We had a wonderful time. We had dancing. We had movies. We had services on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean religious services?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do-- religious services. And they-- in the ballroom there, there was a picture of Adolf Hitler. And naturally, we didn't want to have service in front of it-- his [INAUDIBLE] face on it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we asked the captain to remove it, and he did. People went to see the captain. We had a very good relation with him too. And we were looking forward to our life in Havana. What were the other passengers on the ship like? Same like you, kind of?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all had the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fairly comfortable circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody had paid their own way?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I'm telling you that most people, Jewish people, in Germany were middle class. And I would say the same thing like we here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked hard. We had the same rights, so to speak of, at the beginning for voting, or whatever it is. But this naturally changed afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: All had changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And-- [CLEARS THROAT] We arrived in Havana-- gee, I forgot the date, exactly date-- but it was about five o'clock in the morning. And I was already up.

speaker: interviewer
text: About two weeks? Took about two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- 12 Days.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 Days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like this. I cannot tell you exactly. I was at five o'clock up on deck. And we saw Havana coming in there. We had all the suitcases ready to disembark, because they told us to get ready-- everything ready. We arrive in the harbor of Havana, and the ship docked in the middle of the bay. And they said they had some-- I don't know-- they had to some papers to straighten out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we waited, waited, waited, and nothing happened. The Cuban police came up and around the ship there were some Cuban police, and nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I have some relatives-- my uncle was there already. He came by with a small ship, a boat, and talked to us from far away. That's my first Spanish word I learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manana. He said, tomorrow, manana, manana, you come off. It was for one day, two days, three days, four days-- we stayed in Havana for seven days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like on the ship those seven days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much confusion. We didn't know what's going on. And there was-- actually, we started to worry. There was a lawyer from Breslau and [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. I remember his son, and he had a daughter too, and he was also in Buchenwald. And he was afraid that something will happen. I was playing on deck, and then I see, all of a sudden, a guy running off from the deck there, full of blood, running off. He cut his [? wrist, ?] on both sides, and jumped overboard. Everybody was screaming. And there was a sailor all the way up on the ship painting, and he saw the guy overboard. He jumped over and saved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He saved him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took him to, to Havana, another small boat, put him on the ship, and took him in the hospital. His wife or children were not allowed to disembark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the only one there who strayed from us. There were a few other people who disembarked, but I think they were from some other countries, Czech or something like this. I don't know that anybody from-- with a permit like we had disembarked at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else try to jump off, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since that time, then they organized a committee to watch day and night. Everybody had some time to walk the-- on deck, you know? To make sure nobody else is jumping or something like this. And naturally, the-- the spree00 how you say--

speaker: interviewer
text: The camaraderie between people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not only that, but we all get depressed. We worry about what's going to happen. The captain, even with himself, he changed his suit and put civilian suits and went to see-- try to get to see the president of Cuba to ask him to let his people in. And he was not allowed to see him. In the meantime, I found out that the Joint Distribution Committee got in touch with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --The Cuban government. And they came to see us on board and said, don't worry. We will take care of it, and so on. They negotiated with the Cuban government, and also with Batista. But I didn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let the passengers-- now, you were a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you might-- but did your parents know what was going on? Or were most people just kind of in the dark about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, we were in the dark. Once in a while, we heard from the people who came in a small boat and told us what's going on in the newspaper and so on. But basically, what the Joint did or-- we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they're negotiating with them. I just can tell you what I found out after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But at the time, it was just commotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Commotion and worry. What's going to happen? Because we hear that the government wouldn't let us in. And they wanted a certain amount of money to have us to come in there, which we didn't have no money at all. So after 7 days, the captain got orders from the government to move out from the Cuban harbor, because he had to pay too much money. It needs a certain amount of money you had to pay for it, and the [INAUDIBLE] didn't want to pay no more. So he said, don't worry, we will cruise around the Caribbean, and we find a place for you. Well, the rumor started to go around that they might let us in the Island of Pines. And we send our telegrams all over the world, to Panama, Argentina, the Dominican Republic, to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sent a telegram to Mr. Roosevelt. We didn't get no answer. We sent a telegram to Mrs. Roosevelt to let only the children in. Again, we didn't get no answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: No answer there either.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were very disappointed. Naturally, the, the happiness to leave Germany got to be depressing. Everybody was so depressed. What's going to happen to us? Because we know if you go back to Europe what's going to happen. I may have mentioned something, which I forgot to tell you. A brother of my father in 1938 made a remark against Hitler somehow. Well, he was arrested and sent to the [? hole. ?] In about eight or 10 months later, an SS officer came to my aunt's house with a little box and said, here's your husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was already 1938, we know what they're doing in the, in the concentration camps. So we were worried about--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew what you were running away from.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we-- we were worried about it. So meantime, the telegrams-- no answers. The-- our hopes start to go way down, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- our captain said, I will cruise around the Strait of Florida. Maybe somebody will hear us and will let us out. So we flew-- we flew-- the boat went close to the Florida Strait. And I found out afterward the captain wanted us to come down here to Miami. Let's stop at that point. OK?
